text: The surgeon performs a robotic esophagectomy, focusing on abdominal lymph node dissection and graft formation. The pancreas is visible as the surgeon grasps and removes perigastric and peripancreatic tissue. Dissection is extended around the common hepatic artery, revealing lymph nodes labeled as number eighteen in the AJCC staging system. The left gastric artery is identified and encircled with webbing, followed by dissection along the celiac axis. A green stapler is used to create a long graft, pulling the stomach to achieve the desired length, and removing lymph nodes around the stomach.

The surgeon performs a thoracic procedure involving right recurrent laryngeal nerve dissection, visible alongside the vagus nerve and subclavian artery. The mediastinal pleura is opened and held anteriorly, widening the surgical field. Gentle dissection is carried out around the nerve, removing tissue between the nerve and esophagus, with visible lymph nodes in the right upper thoracic area. The inferior thyroid artery is observed crossing the surgical field, and the thyroid gland is visible upon deeper dissection.

The surgeon uses robotic assistance to remove lymph nodes between the esophagus and recurrent laryngeal nerve from the chest to the neck. The dissection includes cervical paraesophageal lymph nodes, with clear visualization of the left recurrent laryngeal nerve and surrounding structures. The esophagus is separated, and the lymph nodes along the trachea and major airways are removed without damage. The thoracic duct remains intact, and the stomach graft is pulled up to the suture site for anastomosis.
